Robert, the notes were posted over at wine disorder. Todd doesn’t like cross posting so much and I try to respect that.

The two vintages offer different things right now, the 2011 is more supple on the palate but the 2010 has more aromatic complexity. It might be fun to do both. I had them a couple of days apart.

posts.name: N@th@n V@nd€rgrift
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

thread drift–are the 09s drinking well now? Are they as good as touted back then?

Of the recent 2009s that I’ve had, Coudert Tardive continues to improve while I wish I had drank my Lapierre in it’s youthfully exuberant stage. Descombes Morgon VV was silky on the palate, but the nose wasn’t as developed. That’s all I’ve had recently.

posts.name: Richard T r i m p i
posts.cooked:
Nathan V.:

Of the recent 2009s that I’ve had, Coudert Tardive continues to improve while I wish I had drank my Lapierre in it’s youthfully exuberant stage…

Clos de la Roilette impresses me as wonderfully consistent. A bellwether producer and fine QPR.

Completely agree on the 09 Lapierre (realizing we’re a distinct minority). I’ve got 2 bottles left waiting to “come around”. My doubts continue.

RT

posts.name: Len Fox

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

thread drift–are the 09s drinking well now? Are they as good as touted back then?

Most of them still need time, I think. I had a Domaine de la Bonne Tonne Morgon Côte du Py over the weekend and it wasn’t ready yet, but seemed on a good way. Some other 2009s I had recently (Foillard 3.14, Descombes Morgon VV) were delicious, but also not quite where they presumably will be when they’re truly ready to drink. We have 12 different 2009 Morgon Côte du Pys lined up for the end of the month and I’ll report back on how they will have fared.

Recent Cru Beaujolais purchases? No, I’m waiting for the 2013 Château du Moulin-à-Vents and bought some 2014 Domaine Serol L’Incorruptible which taste like Cru Beaujolais, but are from the Côte Roannaise.

posts.name: Richard T r i m p i
posts.cooked:
Kevin Porter:

Ugh - now you tell me!..Thanks for the heads up.

Don’t give up on it. A chacun son gout. There’re plenty of BBers who consider the 09 Lapierre to be the best of the decade and a sleeping superstar.

RT

I grabbed a handful last month:

2009 Terres Dorées/J.P. Brun Moulin-à-Vent (2)

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Nathan V.:
Robert Alfert:
Nathan V.:

2013 Coudert Griffe (after fantastic bottles of 2011 and 2010 in the past 10 days)

Nathan -

Would love to see your notes on the 2011 and 2010. I just popped the 2009 and it’s starting to show really nice promise. I’m sitting on quite a bit of the other two vintages in 750s and Mags. Thoughts on which would be most open for Thanksgiving dinner?

Robert, the notes were posted over at wine disorder. Todd doesn’t like cross posting so much and I try to respect that.

The two vintages offer different things right now, the 2011 is more supple on the palate but the 2010 has more aromatic complexity. It might be fun to do both. I had them a couple of days apart.

I thought lots of people cross-posted. Not sure why that’s an issue, especially when someone does it on two boards that are so fundamentally different: WB v. WD. I guess I could understand not wanting cross-posting by competing, highly-populated boards. Wine Disorder is a very niche, low volume board, though. This board could benefit vastly by some cross-posting from that board. I rarely if ever migrate over there, and have never posted, but it appears that the profile of the participants are closer to me than here. Here, however, is like a marketplace of tastes, ideas and subjects, so generally more interesting to me. I only have time for one board. I guess what I am saying is, post more damn notes here, Nathan, your wine selections are awesome!

posts.cooked: Had the 2014 Roilette base domaine last night at a local restaurant. Amazed to find this wine on an Orlando wine list, just wow. And well priced at $38/bottle. Didn’t spend much time thinking about the wine as I was engaged during dinner, but it was a lovely, approachable Beaujolais, with ripe and sappy red and dark fruits. Could have used greater acidity. Very nice with food, a fine QPR. Say 89 or so on rating. Looking forward to the Cuvee Tardive.

I might have to go revisit this wine. Tried it once and was not a fan one bit. If I recall it seems very “grapey” for lack of better terms. But I keep seeing it pop up, so maybe it was an off bottle or a bad tasting night for me.

i suppose it’s all a matter of reference point. This wine caught my eye on a trip to the region years ago. On this particular trip, I was experiencing quite austere wines which may have psychologically helped liken the wine to my palate.

2013 foillard CDP is what made me realize how stellar Cru B can be. ive had it about 8 times this last year, which is a lot for me for one wine. i now have around 24 bottles between 4 different vintages of foillard CDP/Fleurie/corcelette on the way/in storage. The fleurie is great, but the corcelette has never seemed quite as good to me… maybe the 2014s will be different?

posts.name: suzanne_camhi
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:
scamhi:
Warren Taranow:

2014 Jean Foillard Morgon Eponyme

I just picked this ^ up too

What exactly is ^ this? I saw the offering, and almost jumped, but ultimately passed. Love Foillard but have a fair bit. Was more interested in the Jamet . . . .

From the sales email:

Eponyme is sourced from a tiny parcel in Les Charmes. Just to quickly underscore its rarity, we should mention that there is currently no other listing in the U.S.

It’s early to generalize much about Eponyme, but based on tasting a couple of other producers’ wines from the site and the debut vintage, we feel that we have some sense of the wine. If Côte du Py is rich and wild-cherry-accented and Corcelette has a silky delicacy, Eponyme is delicate, darker fruited and tense. All Foillards have an amazing textural intrigue, but here you get a minerality that’s profoundly tactile. We love the wine’s energetic mineral streak and nuanced floral notes.

posts.name: John Danza
posts.cooked:
1967 J. Thorin Domaine Des Journets Chenas in half bottle.

Technically this is a purchase, just not for storing in my cellar. I was at Bern’s Steakhouse last week and I ordered this off the wine list for $20. The nose was dried red fruits initially, changing to straight dried strawberry after 30 minutes in the decanter. The palate was dried strawberries all the way, with a touch of tannin and decent but not off-putting acidity. Light red in color. Not much life left but actually drank decently with the red snapper I ordered.

posts.name: bob parsons alberta
posts.cooked:
2013 Domaine des Terres Dorees Morgon Jean-Paul Brun.

12.5% alc, medium purple in color. Raspberry and cherry on the nose with some white pepper notes. Good length on entry, soft tannins hardly noticed by fellow taster. Red berries,earthy, medium acidity, not one for the long haul I think. Splash decanted $30 Cdn.

posts.name: Robert S c h n e i d e r
posts.cooked: 2014 Bertrand Fleurie “Cuvée du Chaos.” Delicious and spicy. Not sure it’s $38 delicious, tho.
posts.display_username: Robert S c h n e i d e r

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:

Has anyone actually been to Beujolais? I will be driving from Beaune to Rhone, but on a Sunday, so not trying to visit anyone. Are there any wine shops in the area worth stopping into?

Hi Alan, I’ve been to the Beaujolais many times, but decent wine stores are a rarity down there. The store in Fleurie right by the main square is good, but (as almost all shops) it’s closed on Sundays. Les Comptoirs des Tontons in Beaune has an excellent selection of Beaujolais, but it’s also closed on Sundays. A safe choice would be Antic wine in Lyon, which is open on Sundays.

Took a gamble and got all they had left, not an easy wine to get in the west coast, did not want to pay for shipping for a few bottles cross country.

posts.name: Richard T r i m p i
posts.cooked:
StevenB:

Recent Cru Beaujolais purchases? No, I’m waiting for the 2013 Château du Moulin-à-Vents and bought some 2014 Domaine Serol L’Incorruptible which taste like Cru Beaujolais, but are from the Côte Roannaise.

I tried a bottle of 2015 Domaine Serol Perdriziere Cote Roannaise a couple nights ago. Gamay Saint-Romain grape variety. I found the wine full and fruity (darker spectrum black briar fruit) with some distracting sweetness. It was probably the ripe vintage adding to the sweet syrupy fruit quality. The alcohol was in check and the flavors hadn’t shifted in to raisiny-ness. Good acidity. It reminded me of a light Aussie Shiraz with that double whammy of sweet fruit and bright acid. The retailer was hyping it. Perhaps it’s a different story in cooler vintages. Won’t be reloading this one.

RT
